turns.content: What's the difference between using a channel as an event bus versus wrapping it with Arc and Mutex for shared state? I'm trying to figure out why my CI runner is leaking memory.

The core distinction lies in how ownership propagates through your threads. A plain channel like std::sync::mpsc::Sender naturally enforces a single-producer model, which fits most event bus patterns where only the pipeline orchestrator emits events. If you wrap that sender with Arc<Mutex<>> or similar shared state types, you're introducing contention points that can block execution and increase memory pressure due to guard ownership.
I see, but I'm still hitting retain cycles because my test suite spawns many worker threads holding strong references to the event emitter. How do I break that without changing my data structures?

You need to introduce weak references into your graph of connections. Using std::sync::Weak is the standard way here, letting child workers hold Weak pointers while the parent holds Strong ones. This ensures the parent can still send events, but if a worker thread drops its Strong reference, the emitter becomes unreachable and gets collected automatically.
Okay, so I should use Weak for my subscribers and keep Strong only in the orchestrator? But what about broadcasting to multiple different threads without them all holding strong references?

Exactly, you want a broadcast pattern where the parent maintains Strong ownership of the channel while children hold Weak references. When a child needs to receive events, it uses tokio::sync::mpsc or std::sync::mpsc with a separate receiver per thread, but avoids holding strong references back to the sender itself.
Wait, if I'm using multiple receivers and weak links, won't that fragment my memory usage across threads? Is there a way to keep this simpler without managing so many handles explicitly?

You can simplify by having a single central channel managed by the orchestrator thread, and having all worker threads subscribe via Weak references to that same channel. This avoids creating new channels per thread and keeps memory consistent.
That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how exactly weak references prevent cycles in my actual code structure right now? Can you walk me through a concrete example with the types involved?

Sure, imagine your event emitter is an Arc<struct EventEmitter> that contains a Sender. Each worker thread holds a Weak<EventEmitter>. When the thread drops its Strong reference to the emitter, the weak link remains valid until no strong references exist anywhere in the system, at which point the emitter gets collected and the channel closes automatically.

Got it, so I should replace my current Arc<Mutex<>> setup with an Arc<Sender> plus Weak pointers everywhere else? And avoid using Mutex on the channels themselves since that defeats the purpose of async?

Yes, exactly. Use Arc for shared ownership but avoid Mutex around the channel itself unless you're doing something unusual like parallel sending from multiple threads in your orchestrator. For most event bus patterns, a single Sender with Weak references everywhere else is sufficient and much simpler.

Thanks! I'll refactor my CI runner now using weak references instead of Arc<Mutex>. One last thing though, what happens if the parent process dies unexpectedly? Will the workers still get notified somehow?

If the parent dies, the channel closes and all active receivers will eventually block or return an error when trying to receive. Workers holding Weak links won't crash immediately but will detect that no new events are coming once the sender is dropped.
I'm trying to build a simple event system in Rust using channels, but I can't figure out how to send an event to multiple subscribers without creating retain cycles.

That's a classic gotcha when building observer patterns with channels. You should use broadcast channels for the one-to-many pattern and store subscriber handles in a map where the values are weak references so they don't hold onto the sender. Specifically, you can use std::sync::mpsc::broadcast for broadcasting events to multiple receivers and then wrap your subscriber registration logic around std::rc::Weak or std::cell::RefCell with internal mutability if needed.

Okay, but I'm still getting a retain cycle because my Event struct is holding the channel sender, and when I try to send it through a Weak reference in my subscription list, the compiler complains that I can't move out of an immutable context.

The issue is that you're trying to store a shared ownership of the Sender inside your subscriber state without properly handling the borrow checker. You should separate the event data from the channel itself by using std::sync::Arc for your Event struct and then only hold the weak reference to the broadcast sender in your subscription registry. This way, the Arc prevents the cycle while the Weak allows safe dereferencing when needed.
I see, so I need to wrap the event data in an Arc but keep the channel separate? And what exactly should my subscriber registry look like if I'm using weak references?

Exactly. Your subscriber registry should be a HashMap where keys are unique identifiers and values hold weak references to your subscribers or a collection of them via a RefCell with internal mutability so you can add/remove dynamically without breaking ownership rules.

Imagine I have a game engine where the UI thread needs to notify many subsystems whenever a player character spawns. If I just store pointers in an array, the UI might accidentally keep those references alive and prevent the spawned entities from being garbage collected when they fall off-screen.

That's exactly the classic retain cycle problem in Rust. The solution is to use channels as your event bus, specifically std::sync::mpsc for one-to-many broadcasting or tokio::sync::broadcast if you're using async runtime. You'd send SpawnEvent messages through the channel so subscribers react without holding direct references.

Right, but what about when a subscriber needs to observe state changes that happen in other threads? I want a pattern where the observer doesn't need to move or take ownership of data it only reads from.

You're describing the Observer pattern implemented with weak references. Instead of strong pointers, use Arc<Weak<T>> so subscribers can observe changes without creating cycles. For a game engine this is often done via std::sync::Arc combined with Weak to allow safe broadcast updates.
So I'm thinking of using tokio::sync::broadcast and Arc<Weak<T>> together? But what if the subscriber thread panics or gets dropped unexpectedly?

That's a great catch. If you use tokio::sync::broadcast correctly, it handles receiver drops gracefully. The key is ensuring your SpawnEvent messages are sent with proper synchronization and that weak references can be upgraded safely before being used in the subscriber logic.

I keep reading about using channels as an event bus in Rust tutorials but I still don't get why we need weak references here. I think if I just use a strong reference to the subscriber, everything should work fine and be simpler.

That's a common misunderstanding when you first look at this pattern. Think of it like a notification system where subscribers are people subscribed to your app updates; if they unsubscribe but their memory isn't released yet, they might still try to receive notifications causing crashes. A weak reference acts like an ID card that only works while the person is actually alive in the system. In Rust terms, when you use std::sync::Weak or similar patterns with a channel, you prevent retain cycles because the strong count stays zero until someone explicitly takes ownership of the subscriber.

Okay so I should just replace every Arc<Subscriber> with Weak<Subscriber>? That sounds like it would break my existing code where I need to store who is subscribed for later debugging. What if I want to know exactly which objects are currently listening?

You don't necessarily have to replace everything, but you do need a way to check validity without breaking the cycle. Imagine having a registry that tracks subscribers; when you add someone, you store their ID in a strong reference and also register them in a weak map so you can verify they're still listening later. This way you can query who is subscribed using the weak references safely without forcing the subscriber to stay alive longer than intended.

So I'm right now writing my own channel implementation but I'm getting ownership errors because my subscriber struct holds a strong ref to another component in my game loop which creates the cycle. How do I refactor this specific part?

You need to break that dependency chain by introducing a weak reference holder for your subscribers. Instead of storing Subscriber directly, wrap it in Weak<Subscriber> and maintain a separate Arc<Subscriber> only when you're actively managing its lifecycle. When the game loop component is dropped, the strong count drops to zero, allowing the subscriber to be collected even though the channel still holds the weak reference.
Wait, but then how does the broadcast actually send data? If I'm not holding a strong ref anymore, doesn't that mean the message can't find its way back to the subscribers?

The broadcast mechanism sends messages out while you hold the strong reference during active management; once the sender is dropped or the game loop ends, no more messages are sent. The weak references only prevent retention cycles after the system has stabilized. Think of it like a mailroom where packages are delivered to active subscribers while their status check confirms they haven't left yet.

I think I finally see how this prevents memory leaks in my game loop but I'm still confused about why we can't just use Arc everywhere and manually track who gets dropped. Is there a specific trait or pattern that makes weak refs easier than manual tracking?

The key insight is that Rust's type system enforces these relationships automatically through the borrow checker rather than requiring you to implement manual drop logic yourself. You don't need any special traits beyond standard Arc and Weak types; the compiler handles most of the complexity. Manual tracking often introduces bugs because forgetting a drop or mismanaging lifetimes causes crashes, whereas weak references provide safety by design.

I thought I could just send a message through a channel and have all my workers receive it automatically, similar to how a simple broadcast works in other languages. My code looks like this: let mut sender = Sender::new(); for worker in workers.iter() { worker.start(sender.clone()) }; sender.send(event);

That approach has a fundamental flaw because the Sender type is not designed to be cloned for multiple independent receivers in the way you expect. When you call sender.clone(), you get another mutable handle, but if any of your workers try to send or receive on that shared channel without proper synchronization, you will encounter race conditions. The standard channel API assumes a single owner per direction unless you specifically use different types like MpscSender and MpscReceiver for multiplexed communication.

I see now about the ownership model, but I was hoping to implement an observer pattern where one event triggers multiple listeners without them interfering with each other. Can I just wrap this in a Mutex so that each worker gets its own copy of the data and updates independently?

Using a Mutex around shared mutable state is not the right solution for an event system because it introduces unnecessary contention and blocking when you want independent observers. The correct Rust pattern for this scenario involves using channels as an event bus where producers send events to a broadcast channel, and each subscriber registers itself by receiving from its own dedicated receiver end of that same channel instance.

So I should create one Sender and multiple Receivers? But how do I ensure the receivers don't hold onto the sender in a way that creates retain cycles if they store references to other shared objects?

You are correct about using one Sender with multiple Receivers, but you must be careful about reference ownership. If your listeners store strong references to shared data structures along with their receiver handles, those shared structures will never be dropped until all receivers are dropped, creating a retain cycle. The solution is to use weak references for the shared objects so that listeners can observe state without preventing its drop.
That makes sense, but I don't think Rust has built-in weak references for channels like std::sync::WeakRef. Is there another way to achieve this pattern without manual reference counting?

For channels specifically, you typically don't need explicit weak references because the channel itself manages its own lifecycle through Send and Sync traits. However, if your listeners hold strong references to other shared data, you should use Arc<Weak<T>> or similar patterns for that specific data, not the channel. The event system relies on the fact that once a Sender is dropped, no new events can be sent, which naturally terminates the flow of notifications.

I'm still confused about how to actually set this up without manual reference counting. Should I use something like std::sync::Arc for the receivers themselves or just keep them as regular references?

You should not wrap individual channel receivers in Arc unless you are sharing the receiver itself across threads, but typically each worker owns its own Receiver handle directly. The key is that the Sender is usually shared via Arc<Sender> if you need to clone it, while the Receivers are owned by their respective workers. For your specific use case of an event bus, you might consider crates like async-channel or tokio::sync::broadcast which provide built-in patterns for this exact scenario.

Ah, so I should probably look into tokio::sync::Broadcast instead of trying to implement raw channels manually? That sounds much cleaner. But wait, if I use that crate, how do I prevent retain cycles with the same logic we discussed earlier?

Using tokio::sync::Broadcast is indeed a better approach for your scenario because it handles the cloning and receiving semantics correctly out of the box. To prevent retain cycles with shared data, you should still apply the weak reference pattern to your application's state objects themselves, not to the broadcast channel. The Broadcast type manages its own sender lifecycle, so you only need to worry about weak references for any external data that your listeners might hold strong pointers to.
